To prevent the workflow from processing empty files, add a validation condition to check that the number of rows to import is not equal to 0. This ensures that the workflow only proceeds when valid employee data is present in the file.

Below is the overall view of the completed workflow:

4.8 Execution logs

  • The execution logs for each workflow run can be accessed under the "Run History" tab within the workflow. This provides details on:

    • Execution status (Success, Failed, Skipped)
    • Number of records processed
    • Errors or warnings encountered during execution
    • Timestamp of each run

    Notifications: Once the job is completed, an email notification will be automatically triggered and sent to the respective stakeholders maintained under the "Sharing & Permissions" section of the workflow. This ensures that the relevant team members are promptly informed of:

    • Successful directory sync completions
    • Any failures or errors requiring attention

 

Conclusion

In this blog, we successfully established the foundation for integrating SAP SuccessFactors with Qualtrics by creating an automated Employee Global Directory sync.

Reference:

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-platform/integrating-sap-successfactors-with-qualtrics/integrating-sap-successfactors-with-qualtrics

What's Next?

In Blog 2, we will leverage this directory to configure an automated New Hire Survey - triggering onboarding feedback surveys directly from SuccessFactors events into Qualtrics.

Stay tuned!

entry.title: Understanding Work Schedule in SuccessFactors
entry.content:
I’ll admit it feels a little strange to say this after delivering lots of SuccessFactors projects, but most of my experience has been in EC–WFS–ECP landscapes. In that setup, my involvement with work schedules was usually limited to maintaining a few generic ones in EC while the real complexity lived in the WFS time system, safely handled by my talented colleagues.

But recently, I had to do some Time Tracking work (a module I find fascinating). I didn’t find a lot of documentation on Work Schedule or less than I would have imagined. Naturally, this sparked a desire to dig deeper into the configuration itself. As always when I dig in for something new, I need to understand every detail and every parameter available, how it works and for what business purpose I would use them. So, in this blog, I’m sharing what I’ve learned from my research, with a lot of screenshots to illustrate how the Work Schedule config impacts the time pieces. My goal is to make it easier for you to grasp it all, so you can concentrate on creating cool solutions to complicated requirements.

If you find this useful, don’t forget to hit that little Kudo button at the top. It pays absolutely nothing… but it feeds my ego, which is almost as good.

 

Clock versus Duration: Understanding the Time Recording Variant first!

Before you can choose the right Work Schedule setup, you need to understand the Time Recording Variant. This setting determines how working time is represented in SuccessFactors.

A Time Recording Variant can be configured as:

  • Duration: a total number of hours for a day (8 hours) or
  • Clock: a detailed schedule with start and end times (08h00 AM to 04h00 PM for 8 hours)

 

So… 8 hours versus 8 hours… Isn’t it the same?

Short answer No! It is very different

  • The Duration recording method tells us only how many hours someone works or is absent from work. Time is expressed simply as a total number of hours for the day, without linking those hours to specific times of day. It answers the question “how many hours are worked?” and is ideal for straightforward or flexible work patterns where start and end times aren’t needed.
  • The Clock recording method tells us when someone works or is absent. Time is captured using specific start and end times, allowing you to outline the full structure of a workday, including shifts, breaks, and overnight segments. It answers the question “when exactly does the work happen?”.

 

And why does it matter?

It all comes back to the company’s requirements:

  1. Do we need to know when employees start and finish work or an absence (if it isn’t a full day absence)?
  2. If someone arrives late or leaves early, do we care and do we need to know it?
  3. Do breaks matter to us? Do we need to track when / how long they lasted?
  4. Do we have night, weekend, or after‑hours premiums that depend on the time of day?
  5. Do we need visibility of how many employees were physically on the floor at a specific time?
  6. Do we have shifts that cross midnight and do these need to be captured accurately?

If you are answering YES, then Clock is probably what is best for your case!

 

Is using Clock just better then?

There is no better. While Clock time can sounds like the safer, more complete option, it comes with complexity that many businesses don’t need, can’t maintain, or simply don’t want and a lot of business simply do not need that level of detail and Duration is then a better fit for them.

 

 

Back to our main topic: Work Schedule

A Work Schedule defines the expected working pattern for an employee and serves as the system’s reference for how a normal workday should look. It outlines which days the employee works, how many hours (if Duration) or the detail of all the start and stop time for the day (if Clock). When attendance time or absences are entered, the system can compares them to the Work Schedule to determine regular time, overtime, deviations, and the correct absence deductions.

When creating a Work Schedule in SuccessFactors, you first need to define what the Work Schedule Model will be between:

  • Simple
  • Period
  • Schedule

 

Simple Work Schedule Model

The Simple Work Schedule Model is the most straightforward configuration type.  It is designed for Duration‑based time recording, where employees’ working times are tracked by total hours rather than specific start and end times.

In this model, administrators manually enter the planned working hours for each day, making it an uncomplicated solution for stable, predictable weekly schedules. This simplicity makes it suitable for environments where work patterns do not vary significantly.

 

Period Work Schedule Model

The Period Work Schedule Model is more advanced and flexible than the Simple model, designed to accommodate both duration‑based and clock‑time‑based time recording. It allows organizations to structure work patterns over multi‑day or multi-week or other periods using Day Models, making it ideal for rotating schedules, mixed shift structures, or environments with differentiated working patterns throughout the cycle.

 

Schedule Work Schedule Model

The Schedule Work Schedule Model is designed to reuse an existing Period Work Schedule and repeat its pattern starting from a different Start Date, allowing you to apply the same recurring work pattern across work schedule with less configuration. Instead of rebuilding the same Period Work Schedule structure for every start date scenario, the Schedule Model simply assigns the pattern based on a new reference start date, making it a powerful tool for organizations that need repeating or rotating schedules with flexible start points.

 

Day Model

A Day Model is a standalone object that defines the planned working time for a specific day. It specifies how many hours an employee should work, including segments, start and end times, and breaks. A Period Work Schedule is created by combining multiple Day Models (most of the time… it can be more than just Day Models but let’s keep it simple here). A Day Model can also be reused across different schedules.

When creating a day model, you will need to specify the segment categories between the following (note that I will use Clock Variant in all scenarios):

 

Schedule Working Time:

This is the main segment of a working day. It defines the planned start and end time of the day model in the Clock Variant or the hours expected in the Duration Variant. Only one Scheduled Working Time segment is allowed per day model and it is not possible to create a Schedule working time segment on non-workdays.

When a Day Model is configured only as one Schedule Working Time segment, here it what it looks like on the Timesheet and in the Create Absence Request form:

 

Schedule Unpaid Break:

This segment defines a fixed break during the working day that is deducted from working time. This is about deducting unpaid time from the schedule working time segment.

When a Day Model contains Scheduled Unpaid Break segment, here is what it looks like on the Timesheet and in the Create Absence Request form:

 

Flextime Bandwidth:

The flextime bandwidth extends the working window beyond the scheduled working time so employees can start earlier or finish later within allowed limits. It could also be used to ignore hours done outside that specify window from the timesheet valuation.

When a Day Model contains Flextime Bandwidth segment, here is what it looks like on the Timesheet and in the Create Absence Request form:

 

Night Shift Bandwidth:

This segment is used for cross‑midnight, where an employee starts work late in the evening and finishes the next day. This is really just a Flextime Bandwidth for a Cross-Midnight Shift.

When a Day Model contains Night Shift Bandwidth segment, here is what it looks like on the Timesheet and in the Create Absence Request form:

 

Day Model: Shift Classification

A Day Model Shift Classification is used to categorize a Day Model (or an entire Work Schedule). This classification can later be leveraged in Timesheet Time Valuation, where it becomes possible to extract (or, more precisely, filter) planned time that carry that classification.

For example, if a company pays a $2/hour premium for employees assigned to the Night Shift, adding a shift classification gives us a clear indicator that the employee is scheduled on a Day Model eligible for that premium.

In my view, the name “Shift Classification” is a bit misleading, because the field can be used for far more than shift‑related scenarios. Fully exploring this would require diving deeper into Timesheet Valuation logic… but here’s one way it can be used creatively.

In one of my projects, employees worked on a 28‑day rotation and became eligible for permanency only after completing a certain number of full cycles. To track this, I assigned a shift classification called “End of Rotation” to the Day Model used on the last day of the Work Schedule. In Time Valuation, it then became possible to count how many rotations an employee completed simply by filtering entries where the Day Model had the “End of Rotation” classification via Filter Input Groups (Filter of Shift Classification).

This illustrates how Shift Classification can be used creatively in Valuating time in Time sheet, not just to label shifts and report on it, but to support business logic tied to specific days within a work pattern.

 

Day Model: Rounding Rule

A Day Model can include a grace period rounding rule. This rule helps round out small differences between the scheduled start or end time and the employee’s actual clock‑in or clock‑out time. Instead of counting a few minutes early or late as extra work or a violation, the recorded time is automatically rounded to the planned start or end time. For example, if a 10‑minute grace period is defined, any punch that occurs within 10 minutes before or after the scheduled start time is rounded back to the official start time. This Rounding is created within its own object and then assigned to the Day Model in the Rounding field. Once selected, the system applies the defined rounding logic consistently to all time recordings linked to that Day Model.

 

Day Model: Day Model Usage Types

I have decided that I will create a blog dedicated to Day Model Day Variant soon to make this post a bit lighter and removing the need to talk about Holiday Calendar in this one. Therefore, here I will just explain briefly the concept and add the link to that promised post when it is completed 😉!

A Day Model also needs to define the day model usage type meaning if the day is a normal (or regular) working day or if it is a day variant that will be used define specific reduction of planned working time when a working day is on a Holiday.

Two Day Model Usage Types

  1. Regular Day Model (for Work Schedule assignment): Used for standard working days or non-working days. You define the normal planned hours—for example, 8 hours, or 08:00 to 17:00 with a 12:00 to 13:00 lunch break. Regular Day Model can be assigned as a day Period Work Schedule configuration.
  2. Variant for Partial Holidays (for Day Model assignment): This type of day is not directly assigned to a work schedule, and it applies only in special situations such as half‑day holidays, shortened days before a holiday, or any custom reduced‑hours scenario because of Holiday. A Variant for Partial Holiday Day Model can be assigned as a Variant day in a Regular Day Model configuration.

In summary, a Regular Day Model defines standard daily working hours, while a Variant Day Model defines reduced‑hour versions to be used when the regular day model is on a holiday.

Here is what happens on the Work Schedule when on a Partial Holiday when the Day Variant is configured:

 

Other things to know about Work Schedules in SuccessFactors…

We’ve covered the main configuration elements of Work Schedules and how they (or they could) influence Time Sheet, Time Valuation, and Time‑Off in SuccessFactors. Before closing the topic, here are additional features that are useful and should not be overlooked.

 

Work Schedule Searchable Field

If your company is beautifully complex 😉 and happens to operate with a huge number of Work Schedules, using the searchable field option in the Work Schedule configuration could make your life a bit easier.

With this option, it is possible to search for the right Work Schedule with additional key words when assigning it to an employee, instead of scrolling endlessly through a long list or having to know the exact code. It’s a small configuration choice that can significantly improve efficiency.

 

Assigning Temporary Work Schedule

Temporary Work Schedules are extremely helpful when an employee needs to follow a different work pattern for a short, defined period, for example, covering a colleague for a couple of weeks, switching to evenings for two weeks, or working reduced hours temporarily. Instead of modifying the employees’ permanent work schedule in Job Information (which would create additional slices to the job history), it is possible to assign a Temporary Work Schedule directly in the Time Management section. You simply set the start and end dates, and the system automatically uses the temporary pattern for that period before returning to the original schedule. This approach keeps Job Information clean, avoids unnecessary data maintenance, and provides full flexibility without additional configuration objects.

 

Assigning Individual Work Schedule

When an employee has a very specific working pattern that doesn’t match any existing Work Schedule, such as a custom arrangement, irregular hours, accommodation, or a short‑term one‑off pattern, it is possible to use the Individual Work Schedule option. This allows to manually define the daily working hours directly on the Time management. Just like temporary schedules, this option prevents the need to repeatedly update the Work Schedule field in Job Information, avoiding time‑slice complexity and ensuring the core employment data remains stable.

 

Conclusion

Time is complex… and that’s what makes it fun!

If this blog helped you learn something new, or simply made Work Schedules make more sense, then mission accomplished. Time Tracking has so much potential, and the more of us who truly understand it, the cooler and more elegant the solutions we can build together.

My hope is that this little guide gives you clarity, inspiration, and a few Aha! moments along the way.

Thanks for reading… and happy scheduling!
Stéphanie

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors Integration with Qualtrics - Part 2: Automating the New Hire Survey
entry.content:
In this blog, we'll configure an event-driven integration that automatically triggers a New Hire Survey when a new employee joins the organization. No manual participant uploads, Just a seamless, automated experience.

What You'll Learn

  • How to set up an event-based trigger using the Intelligent Services Center (ISC)
  • How to configure the Manage Qualtrics Integrations screen for email surveys
  • How the integration connects to the Lifecycle Project in Qualtrics
  • Why the Global Directory from Part 1 is essential for this to work

Prerequisites

Before you begin, make sure the following are in place:

 

#PrerequisiteDetails
1Part 1 CompletedEmployee directory sync is active and employees are flowing into the Qualtrics EX Global Directory
2Qualtrics Lifecycle ProjectThe Qualtrics consultant has created the New Hire Onboarding Survey as a Lifecycle Project and shared the Project ID
3Register Qualtrics in SuccessFactorsQualtrics authentication must be enabled in the Integration Service Registration Center. This is a one-time setup that allows SuccessFactors to communicate with Qualtrics.
4OAuth ConfigurationQualtrics API credentials (OAuth client) are configured in SuccessFactors
5Admin PermissionsAccess to Intelligent Services Center, Manage Qualtrics Integrations, and Integration Center in SuccessFactors

Architecture Overview

The flow works like this:

  1. A new hire is created in Employee Central
  2. The Intelligent Services Center/ISC detects the new hire event and waits 7 days after the start date
  3. After 7 days, the Manage Qualtrics Integrations email survey integration fires
  4. The integration sends the employee identifier to Qualtrics via API
  5. Qualtrics looks up the employee in the Global Directory (synced in Part 1)
  6. If found, the employee is added as a participant to the Lifecycle Project
  7. The survey invitation is sent automatically.

Step-by-Step Configuration

Step 1: Enable Qualtrics Authentication in Integration Service Registration Center

This is a one-time setup that allows SuccessFactors to communicate with the Qualtrics platform. If this has already been done for your instance, you can skip this step.

Navigation: Admin Center → Integration Service Registration Center

Step 2: Verify the Lifecycle Project in Qualtrics

Before configuring anything in SuccessFactors, confirm with your Qualtrics consultant that:

  • The Lifecycle Project (New Hire Onboarding Survey) has been created
  • The Project ID has been shared with you (e.g., TS_XXXXXXXXXX)
  • The survey is designed, tested, and ready to receive participants
  • The invitation email template and reminder settings are configured within the project

Note: The survey design, email templates, reminder schedule, and dashboard are all configured by the Qualtrics consultant within the Qualtrics platform. This blog focuses on the SAP SuccessFactors side of the integration.

Step 3: Create a Business Rule and Configure the Intelligent Services Center (ISC)

The Intelligent Services Center is where you define what event should trigger the integration and when it should fire. But first, you need to create a business rule that captures the new hire event.

Step 3a: Create the Business Rule

Before configuring the ISC event, you need to create a business rule that identifies when a new hire event occurs in Employee Central.

Navigation: Admin Center → Configure Business Rules

  1. Create a new business rule
  2. Define the rule to capture the hire event from Employee Central
  3. This rule acts as the filter - it tells the ISC exactly which employee events should trigger the survey integration

SAP Help Reference: For detailed steps on creating the business rule for email surveys, refer to the official SAP documentation: Creating Business Rule for Email Surveys

Tip: The business rule defines the "what" (which events to capture), while the ISC event subscription defines the "when" (timing of the trigger). Both work together to ensure the right employees receive the survey at the right time.

Step 3b: Configure the ISC Event Subscription

Once the business rule is created, configure the event subscription in the Intelligent Services Center.

Navigation: Admin Center → Intelligent Services Center

  1. Create a new event subscription
  2. Configure the following:

Setting Value

CategoryEmployee Central: Employee Recruitment
SourceIntegration Center
Timing7 Days After Effective Date
Business Rule(The business rule created in Step 3a)

       3. Save the event rule

 

What this does: The ISC monitors Employee Central for new hire events. When a new hire record is created and matches the business rule, the ISC doesn't fire immediately - it waits 7 days after the employee's effective start date before triggering the integration. This delay is configurable based on your organization's needs.

Step 4: Configure the Email Survey in Manage Qualtrics Integrations

This is where you create and configure the email survey integration that links the ISC event to the Qualtrics Lifecycle Project.

Navigation: Admin Center → Manage Qualtrics Integrations → Email Surveys

Click + Create New Integration and fill in the following:

#FieldValue
1Integration NameSF_NewHire
2Product AreaEmployee Central
3Event NameEmployee Recruitment
4Survey RecipientEmployee
5Business Scenario Name[Employee Central] Qualtrics: New Hire
6OAuth Configuration(Configured in Step 4b)
7Rule Configuration(Business rule from Step 3a)
8Survey Info - Project ID(Lifecycle Project ID from Qualtrics)

Fields 1–4 are self-explanatory. Let's look at the ones that need more detail:

Step 4a: Business Scenario Name

FieldValue
Business Scenario Name[Employee Central] Qualtrics: New Hire

This links the integration to the pre-delivered Integration Center business scenario that handles the data transfer to Qualtrics.

This is a standard pre-delivered business scenario. You don't need to create it - it becomes available automatically when the Qualtrics integration is enabled in your SuccessFactors instance.

Step 4b: OAuth Configuration

FieldValue
OAuth Configuration(Select your Qualtrics OAuth client)

The OAuth configuration provides the secure connection between SuccessFactors and your Qualtrics instance. This must be set up before you can activate the integration.

If the OAuth configuration has not been created yet:

SAP Help Reference: Follow the official SAP documentation for step-by-step instructions: Creating Outbound OAuth Configurations for Qualtrics APIs for Email Survey Integrations

Once created, return to the Edit Integration screen and select the OAuth configuration from the dropdown.

Step 4c: Rule Configuration

FieldValue
Rule Configuration(Select the business rule created in Step 3a)

This links the business rule you created in Step 2a. The rule acts as a filter - only events that match the rule (i.e., new hire events) will trigger the survey.

Step 4d: Survey Info - Project ID

FieldValue
Survey Info - Project IDTS_XXXXXXXXXX

This tells the integration which Qualtrics survey project should receive the participant.

Where to find the Project ID:

MethodHow
URL BarOpen the Lifecycle Project in Qualtrics. The Project ID is in the URL: https://yourorg.qualtrics.com/xxxx/ProjectID=TS_XXXXXXXXXX

Important: Use the Lifecycle Project ID, not a regular survey ID. The integration is designed to work with Qualtrics Lifecycle or Employee Engagement projects.

Step 4e: Review and Activate

Step 5: Activate the business scenario

This links the integration to the pre-delivered business scenario.

FieldValue
Business Scenario Name[Employee Central] Qualtrics: New Hire

Note: This is a standard pre-delivered business scenarios. You don't need to create it - it's automatically available when Qualtrics integration is enabled.

Step 6: Configuration on the Qualtrics Side

 

Survey DesignQuestions, logic, branding
Invitation EmailEmail template with personalized fields (name, department, etc.) pulled from the Global Directory
RemindersAutomatic follow-up emails if the employee hasn't completed the survey (e.g., after 3 days, 7 days)
Survey ExpirationHow long the survey stays open
DashboardReporting and analytics for HR leaders
AnonymityConfidentiality and access control settings

Reminders are configured entirely within the Qualtrics Lifecycle Project. Once a participant is added by our integration, Qualtrics manages the full survey lifecycle - invitation, reminders, expiration - automatically.

6.1 Create a New Lifecycle Project

Create a new Lifecycle Project in Qualtrics for your onboarding survey.

6.2 Build and Publish the Survey

Once published, a Project ID will be generated. This ID is used in Step 4d of the Integration Center flow configuration.

6.3 Configure Message Automation

Step 1: Navigate to the Message Automation Panel

In your Qualtrics Lifecycle Project, open the Messages tab.

This is where all survey invites, reminders, and automation conditions are configured.

Step 2: Select the Automation Condition

For most scenarios, choose:

"All participants who haven't received the survey invite yet."

This ensures that as new employees sync into Qualtrics via the integration, they automatically receive the survey invitation - no manual checks required.

You may also select:

  • Custom Participant Groups — if your use case requires targeting specific individuals
  • Metadata-based Filters — to filter based on participant attributes (department, location, etc.)
  • Multiple Conditions — depending on your business rules

Step 3: Configure the Email Content

After selecting the automation logic, build your email message:

  • Subject Line - Clear and engaging
  • Body Text  - Personalized using dynamic fields
  • Branding - Company logo, colors, tone
  • Dynamic Fields - Pull employee-specific data

Step 4: Set Up Automation Frequency and Timing

Configure the schedule for survey distribution:

Setting Description

FrequencyHow often the survey invite should be triggered (e.g., daily, weekly)
Time of DayWhat time the message should be sent
Time ZoneApplicable time zone for delivery

 

Step 7: Understand What Happens Behind the Scenes

Once the integration is active, here's what happens every time a new hire is created:

Day 0

  • New hire record created in Employee Central

Day 0–1

  • Part 1 daily sync adds the employee to the Qualtrics EX Global Directory

Day 7

  • ISC event fires based on the business rule
  • Integration sends the employee identifier to Qualtrics
  • Qualtrics looks up the employee in the Global Directory
  • Employee is added as a participant to the Lifecycle Project
  • Survey invitation email is sent to the new hire

Key Dependency: The employee must exist in the Qualtrics Global Directory before the Day 7 trigger fires. This is why Part 1 (Employee Directory Sync) is a critical prerequisite. The 7-day delay provides a natural buffer - the daily sync ensures the employee is in the directory well before Day 7.

 

Step 8: Verify the Integration

After activation, test the integration with a test employee:

  • Create a test new hire in Employee Central (or use an existing test employee in a sandbox)

             Employee was hired on 2nd March.

 

  • Wait for Part 1 sync to confirm the employee appears in the Qualtrics Global Directory
  • Trigger the ISC event (in a test environment, you may be able to trigger it manually without waiting 7 days)

  • Check the Lifecycle Project in Qualtrics to confirm the employee appears as a participant

  • Check the email to confirm the survey invitation was received

 

CheckpointWhat to verify
Global DirectoryEmployee appears with correct attributes
Lifecycle ProjectEmployee listed as a participant
Email InboxSurvey invitation received
Survey LinkSurvey opens correctly with personalized content

 

  • If the employee doesn't exist in the global directory, the sync will fail with below error.

 

Conclusion

This integration between SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central and Qualtrics automates the onboarding survey process — ensuring every new hire receives timely feedback requests without manual intervention.

Key Takeaways

  • Automation - Eliminates manual survey distribution using ISC rules and Integration Center
  • Timeliness - Captures new hire sentiment at the right moment
  • Accuracy - Employee data flows directly from EC to Qualtrics with no manual entry
  • Scalability - Works seamlessly irrespective of hire counts
  • Error Handling - Always account for edge cases like missing participants in Qualtrics

By combining the power of SAP SuccessFactors Integration Center, Intelligent Services, and Qualtrics, organizations can build a seamless, data-driven onboarding experience - capturing valuable employee feedback from Day 1.

Thanks for taking time to read..!

Coming Up Next

Blog 3: SAP SuccessFactors Integration with Qualtrics - Part 3: LMS Learning/Training Feedback

We'll explore how to trigger training feedback surveys automatically when an employee completes a learning course in SuccessFactors Learning - capturing real-time insights to continuously improve your L&D programs.

Stay tuned!

entry.title: 'Level in Org' field in SuccessFactors via API/Integration Centre
entry.content:
Scenario:

In SuccessFactors Employee Central, Level in Org is a standard field that shows an employee's reporting level (i.e. number of managers or positions above the employee) in an organization. It is a field that is available for reporting purposes and can be accessed in Canvas reports under the entity User → Users Team Info → Level in Org or it can also be calculated in Story Reports using the Create Calculated Column. This field is quite useful for understanding organizational hierarchy and supports various reporting needs. However, despite its usefulness, the standard Level in Org field has several limitations. For one, it is not available via OData API so cannot be queried using OData queries for integrations with third party systems. Additionally, the standard field in Employee Central is evaluated based on Reporting hierarchy rather than Position hierarchy, which will not help in case Employee Central has Position Management implemented, and customer wants to report on Position hierarchy.

The following visual shows how Level in org can be different for the same employee when using Reporting Hierarchy v/s Position Hierarchy.

To address this gap and get the right level in org based on Position Hierarchy, this blog demonstrates how to derive the correct Level in Org based on Position Hierarchy using an integration built in Integration Centre (IC). 

Pre-requisite

Before starting, you must know the total number of levels in your organization. This value will be used in the integration logic.
For this demonstration, we assume the organization has 7 levels and that the topmost position of CEO starts at level 0.

Solution:

  1. Navigate to Integration Centre in Employee Central.
  2. Create a new integration with:
    • Source: SuccessFactors
    • Destination: SFTP
    • Format: CSV
  3. Select JobInfo as the starting entity.
  4. On the field mapping page, map key fields such as Employee ID, First Name, and Last Name from their respective entities.
  5. Click Add Calculated Field.
  6. Add an IF THEN ELSE condition                                                                                                                                                                                     
    • In Parameter 1, click Associate Field and navigate from the employee’s position to parent position 7 times (based on the number of organizational levels).
    • Now check whether the 7th position is not blank.
    • If it is not blank, populate the field value as 7.
    • If it is blank, populate the field value as a hyphen.
  7. Do the IF THEN ELSE logic above a total of 7 times (equal to number of levels in the org) and do the following in each subsequent check:
    • Reduce the number of times you need to navigate from employee's position to parent position by one (e.g., 2nd check should check if the 6th parent position is not blank, 3rd check should check if the 5th parent position is not blank and so on). In essence, if the Nth parent position exists, populate the value as N; otherwise, populate a hyphen into the field value.
  8. At the end of the 7th IF THEN ELSE check, field value contains a string consisting of hyphens and numbers.
  9. After the 7th IF THEN ELSE check, apply the following check as shown in the screenshot below to extract the correct Level in Org value for the employee.

     

  10. Sample value shown in the Level in Org field.

Conclusion:

By using Integration Centre and calculated fields, you can successfully derive an employee’s Level in Org based on Position Hierarchy. This approach overcomes the limitations of the standard field and ensures consistency with organizational structure, even when positions are vacant. Once populated, the Level in Org value can be securely transferred to downstream vendor systems via SFTP or accessed by middleware solutions for further querying via API.

Please let me know what you think of this solution and feel free to share your questions or feedback in the comments section.

Thanks and Regards

Rahul Soneji

entry.title: Understanding the different ways to initiate the internal hire process in Onboarding
entry.content:
Internal mobility is one of the most powerful tools that an organisation has to retain talent, support career growth, and fill roles efficiently. But in SAP SuccessFactors, “internal hire” can mean many different scenarios — and each scenario behaves differently across Recruiting, Onboarding, and Employee Central.

Here we (myself, @NathanWilkinson and @Angela_Romano) will walk through 9 internal hire scenarios including the advantages, impacts, and decision points.

Before diving into the scenarios, it’s important to align on a few assumptions:

  • You’re using SuccessFactors Recruiting (RCM) and Employee Central (EC).
  • Event Reason Derivation is active in EC.
  • When we say “onboarding process”, we’re referring to any onboarding or crossboarding variant — not a single, universal workflow.

To get started, let's look at the standard process for an external onboarding process and use this as a contrast to the internal process: 

The Standard External Hire

  1. Recruiting (RCM) initiates onboarding.
  2. Onboarding (ONB) collects data and tasks.
  3. Manage Pending Hires (MPH) receives the candidate. This can be processed automatically.
  4. Employee Central (EC) creates a future-dated employment record.

 

This is the smoothest, most automated path which comes as standard out of the box for external candidates. Below we will discuss the 9 internal hire scenarios:

 

1. Internal Hire in the Same Legal Entity (our baseline)

This is the most common scenario and comes as standard out of the box.

Key considerations include:

  • EC will insert a future-dated row that is visible on the EC profile with the correct permissions
  • Workflows only show changed fields, not the overall view
  • Recruitment may not have all the data needed to trigger EC rules depending on complexity
  • Many customers want to review job and compensation data before approval.

In this flow, onboarding is initiated from RCM, but MPH is not used. Once the EC change is saved or approved, event reason derivation kicks in and the onboarding process can begin.

However, in our experience sometimes customers want an alternative to this standard. Below find just one of the processes we have implemented as part of solutioning on projects:

 

2. Internal Hire in the Same Legal Entity Using MPH

This variation gives you more control of what data you see.

  • An eligibility rule prevents onboarding from triggering automatically.
  • The hire lands in Manage Pending Hires, where data can be reviewed and edited.
  • Once submitted, EC creates a future-dated row and triggers workflows.
  • Onboarding can still be initiated after EC approval.

This approach is ideal when you want a checkpoint before data hits EC.

Please see  @NathanWilkinson's blog on this topic and how to configure the steps: https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/utilising-the-define-eligibility-for-onboarding-initiation-rule/ba-p/14251572

This brings us to our third process: 

3. Internal Move Initiated Directly in Employee Central

Not all internal moves start in Recruiting and we need to be able to initiate Onboarding via EC.

For promotions or internal transfers initiated by HR:

  • The employee does not apply in RCM.
  • A workflow approves the EC change.
  • Once approved, an onboarding or crossboarding process can still be triggered.

 

This is useful for moves requiring new documents or compliance steps where you may not need a full data collection but you do require an onboardee to complete compliance information. In this case you could have a process variant with fewer steps and streamline the process. 

 

4. Country Transfer (Different Legal Entity), Not a Global Assignment

This scenario covers when an employee is transferring from one legal entity to another but not on a global assignment. They need to be rehired which ensures compliance and proper separation between employment records.

 

  • Onboarding triggers a rehire check.
  • The employee must have a termination date before the new hire date.
  • After “Rehire on New Employment,” the employee receives a legal entity transfer email.
  • They complete onboarding using the Employment profile switcher.
  • Please be aware that the employee will require ESS access to do this. 

Please see @NathanWilkinson’s blog post on this topic: https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/using-successfactors-onboarding-for-country-transfers-without-recruiting/ba-p/14315749

On the contrary, when you are using a Global Assignment from Recruiting, we are able to do the following: 

5. Global Assignment Initiated from Recruiting

When a global assignment is created via RCM:

  • The hire lands in MPH from Recruitment.
  • A global assignment record is added once approved.
  • Onboarding begins, but Final Review is not supported.
  • The employee completes onboarding using the profile switcher.

Alternatively, if you are triggering the Global Assignment directly within EC, we can do the following:

6. Global Assignment Initiated directly in EC

Many organisations have mobility teams that manage assignments outside of Recruiting so often this is processed directly in to Employee Central.

  • A global assignment is added directly in EC.
  • Once approved, onboarding begins.
  • Final Review is not currently supported.
  • The employee completes onboarding via the employment profile switcher.

 

Similar to global assignments are concurrent employments. We can manage these in two ways; either via RCM or directly in EC.

7. Concurrent Employment Initiated from Recruiting

Similar to global assignments:

 

  • The hire is marked as Concurrent Hire in RCM.
  • The candidate lands in Manage Pending Hires.
  • EC adds the concurrent employment once approved.
  • Onboarding begins, again without Final Review.

8. Concurrent Hire Initiated Without Recruiting

This mirrors scenario 7 but starts in EC.

 

 

  • The concurrent hire lands in MPH.
  • Once approved, EC adds the new employment.
  • Onboarding begins.
  • Final Review is not supported.

Lastly, often we want to initiate an onboarding for contingent workers who then become employees. We may want them to sign a document, complete a compliance process etc. Here is the process of how that works: 

9. Changing Employment Type (e.g. Contingent Worker → Employee)

EC does not allow flipping a contingent worker to an employee.

  • The worker must be terminated and rehired.
  • The process resembles a country transfer in that we rehire them
  • A termination date must exist before the new hire date.
  • Onboarding is completed via the profile switcher.
  • ESS access is required but does not affect licensing.

I hope this blog has been helpful in giving you an overview of all the internal hire scenarios we have in Onboarding. The key message to take away from this blog is that when you are building your internal hire processes - it is essential to think about the bigger picture of the process from start to finish including Recruiting, Onboarding and Employee Central.

entry.title: How SAP's AI copilot is moving from helper to agentic layer that reasons and acts inside HR workflow
entry.content:
Your organization likely spent the last two years deploying conversational AI as a convenience layer: a chat window that answers leave balance questions and helps employees find the right form. Useful. Incremental. Already obsolete.

The shift underway is not from chatbot to better chatbot. It is from tool to colleague. SAP's Joule is moving from a system that responds to one that reasons, plans, and acts, often before anyone asks it to. The implications for HR operating models are far more disruptive than most organizations have internalized.

Deloitte's State of AI in the Enterprise 2026 report found that nearly three-quarters of companies plan to deploy agentic AI within two years. Yet only 21% report having a mature governance model for autonomous agents.[1] That gap between ambition and readiness is where the real risk sits.

This piece examines what agentic AI actually means for HR, how Joule's architecture is evolving to deliver it, and what organizations need to get right before agents start making decisions alongside their people.

The shift from copilot to co-worker

The terminology matters, because the technology has outgrown the language most organizations use to describe it.

A chatbot retrieves information. You ask a question; it returns an answer. The interaction is bounded, reactive, and stateless.

A copilot augments human action. It drafts, summarizes, recommends. It operates within a single workflow at a user's request. Most of what organizations call "AI in HR" today lives here.

An agent does something categorically different. It reasons over a goal, decomposes it into steps, gathers information from multiple systems, makes decisions within defined guardrails, and takes action, often across process boundaries. Critically, it retains context, learns from outcomes, and can initiate work without being prompted.

SAP announced Joule in September 2023 as a generative AI copilot.[2] In the roughly two and a half years since, it has evolved into an agentic platform. The distinction is not academic. When Joule shifts from answering "What is my leave balance?" to proactively telling a manager "Three of your five critical successors have development plans that are stalled. Here's what I recommend," that is not a better chatbot. That is a new kind of organizational actor.

What makes agentic AI different

Deloitte's Tech Trends 2026 report frames the distinction sharply: organizations must begin treating AI agents as a "silicon-based workforce" that complements the human workforce, with dedicated management frameworks for onboarding, performance tracking, and cost management.[3] The metaphor is intentional. Agents are not features to be toggled on. They are workers that happen to run on silicon, and they require governance, oversight, and integration into team structures just as any new hire would.

Most organizations remain early in this transition. While 30% are exploring agentic options and 38% are piloting solutions, only 14% have solutions ready for deployment, and a mere 11% are actively using these systems in production.[4] Legacy system integration, data architecture constraints, and the absence of enterprise-grade governance remain the primary barriers.

For HR, the distinction between copilot and agent carries operational weight. A copilot helps a manager draft a performance review. An agent gathers the employee's goal progress, recent feedback, learning completions, and skills data, synthesizes it into talking points, and suggests follow-up actions like updating a development goal or scheduling a coaching conversation. The manager's role shifts from data assembly to decision-making.

Joule's architecture: why embedded matters

Most enterprise AI copilots sit on top of business applications. They access data through APIs, generate responses in a separate interface, and operate at arm's length from the processes they are meant to improve. This architecture creates a fundamental ceiling: the AI can inform decisions but cannot participate in them.

Joule's architecture is different in a way that matters operationally. It is embedded directly into SAP Business Suite, not bolted on, but woven into the transaction layer of SuccessFactors, S/4HANA, Ariba, Concur, and SAP Analytics Cloud.[5] This means Joule operates within the same security model, the same role-based permissions, and the same workflow logic that governs every human user.

This design choice addresses a critical challenge: nearly half of organizations cite data searchability (48%) and data reusability (47%) as obstacles to their AI automation strategy.[4] By operating within SAP's data layer rather than above it, Joule avoids the integration bottleneck that hampers many enterprise AI deployments.

SAP states that Joule now supports 80% of the most-used tasks across SAP systems, is available in 11 languages, and integrates with Microsoft 365 Copilot, allowing users to access SAP intelligence directly from Outlook and Teams without navigating to SuccessFactors.[6] The Microsoft integration signals that agents are no longer confined to the system they were built in.

The integration with SAP's existing access controls is worth emphasizing. An AI that recommends a successor from outside your permissions context is a liability. An AI that respects the same role-based permission structure as your HRBP, seeing only what that role should see, acting only within those boundaries, is something an organization can actually govern.

The agent roster: what Joule does in HR today

It is easy to dismiss agents as vaporware if you only read roadmap slides. Here is what has shipped and what has firm delivery dates.

Performance and Goals Agent (generally available, November 2025). This was the first Joule Agent purpose-built for HR.[7] Rather than waiting for a manager to ask for performance data, the agent proactively gathers goals, achievements, feedback, and review history for each direct report. It synthesizes this into personalized discussion points and suggests follow-up actions: schedule a one-on-one, update a development goal, adjust a rating based on new evidence. The agent transforms performance conversations from administrative exercises into strategic coaching moments.

Four additional agents (expected general availability, May 2026):[8]

The Career and Talent Development Agent automates succession planning and helps managers identify future leaders. It reasons over existing succession plans, prompts managers for updates when plans go stale, recommends successors based on skills and experience data, and generates personalized development plans. The operative word is "reasons", this is not a report. It is an agent that evaluates the quality of your succession pipeline and acts when it finds gaps.

The HR Service Agent functions as a first line of response for employee questions about company policies, benefits, and processes. Before routing a question to a human, it searches company policy documents and knowledge bases. If it cannot resolve the query, If it cannot resolve the query, it raises a ticket in SAP SuccessFactors Enterprise Service Management.

The People Intelligence Agent connects workforce analytics in SAP Business Data Cloud with Joule's conversational interface. HR leaders can ask questions in natural language, about attrition risk, skills distribution, or capability gaps, and receive visual analytics with recommendations, not just data tables.

The Payroll Agent addresses one of the most common sources of employee frustration: understanding pay. Building on SAP's existing Explain Pay feature in Joule, which integrates data from SuccessFactors time tracking and reportedly reduces pay-related resolution time by up to 50%, the Payroll Agent extends this capability to both employees and payroll administrators, providing natural-language explanations for pay changes connected to specific time entries like overtime, absences, or shift differentials.[9]

At SAP Connect 2025, SAP announced more than a dozen new Joule Agents spanning finance, procurement, supply chain, and HR, part of a broader expansion that brought the total to more than 40 agents across SAP's portfolio.[10] The pattern across all of them is consistent: each agent is domain-specific, operates within enterprise security boundaries, and is designed to reason and act, not just respond.

Additionally, SAP has introduced a workforce knowledge network that integrates third-party content from industry experts, including G-P (Globalization Partners) and The Josh Bersin Company, directly into Joule, enabling agent-to-agent interaction that grounds recommendations in external research and best practices.[11]

Joule Studio: building custom agents

Ready-made agents address common workflows. But every organization has processes that are unique, and this is where Joule Studio enters the picture.

Joule Studio, a capability within SAP Build, reached general availability for skill building in July 2025 and for agent building in January 2026.[12] It allows organizations to create custom Joule agents and skills using a low-code interface, grounded in SAP's Knowledge Graph and business data.

Two capabilities make Joule Studio strategically significant.

First, Model Context Protocol (MCP) support allows custom agents to connect with external tools and non-SAP systems through an open standard.[13] This is not a proprietary integration framework. An HR agent built in Joule Studio can pull compensation benchmarking data from a third-party system and combine it with internal SuccessFactors data to make a recommendation, breaking down the data silos that consistently impede AI value.

Second, multi-agent orchestration allows agents to function as tools for one another. Joule can invoke a recruiting agent and a workforce planning agent in sequence to solve a problem that spans both domains, identifying a skills gap, finding internal candidates, and flagging external hiring needs in a single workflow.[14] This coordination is what distinguishes an agent ecosystem from a collection of disconnected tools.

Early adopters are building production-grade agents quickly. At SAP's Hack2Build event in January 2026, partner teams, including Capgemini, which described Joule Studio as enabling connectivity across SAP and non-SAP environments in hybrid landscapes, created functional custom agents in under seven days.[15]

The governance gap

The capability is moving fast. The governance is not.

The data on this point is unambiguous. While 74% of companies expect to use agentic AI within two years, only 21% have a mature model for governance of autonomous agents.[1] The risks that concern organizations most all relate to governance: data privacy and security (73%), legal and regulatory compliance (50%), and governance capabilities and oversight (46%).[1]

For HR, the governance challenge is acute. AI agents in HR make decisions that affect people's careers, compensation, development opportunities, and access to roles. An agent that recommends successors shapes who gets developed. An agent that answers policy questions defines how policy is interpreted. An agent that explains pay determines whether employees trust their employer.

These are not edge cases. They are the core of what HR does.

A minimum viable governance model for agentic AI in HR should address several critical dimensions:

Permissions inheritance. Role-based permissions must extend cleanly to agents, if a manager cannot see certain employee data, their agent cannot either. SAP has built this into Joule's architecture through principal propagation, meaning the user's identity is passed end-to-end so that any agent action runs within that user's security context.[5]

Human-in-the-loop checkpoints. High-stakes decisions, succession nominations, compensation recommendations, talent mobility, require defined approval points. Joule Studio enables human-in-the-loop by default.[12]

Auditability. Organizations must be able to explain why an agent made a specific recommendation. This is particularly important as regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act impose transparency requirements on high-risk AI systems.

Bias monitoring. When agents influence talent decisions at scale, bias monitoring must be operationalized, not just discussed. This is especially critical for agents involved in succession planning, career development, and internal mobility recommendations.

Platform guardrails are necessary but not sufficient. The harder work is organizational: defining which decisions agents can make autonomously, which require human approval, and how to measure whether agents are making those decisions fairly.

The economic case

The investment case for agentic AI in HR is strengthening, but executives should approach the numbers with appropriate nuance.

SAP's Value of AI Report, conducted with Oxford Economics in 2025, found that organizations are seeing an average 16% ROI on AI investments (approximately $4.7 million), with expectations for that to nearly double to 31% within two years. Seventy-nine percent expect positive ROI in less than three years.[16]

However, Deloitte's research provides important counterweight. Most organizations achieve satisfactory ROI on AI use cases within two to four years, significantly longer than the seven-to-twelve–month payback period typically expected for technology investments.[17] And 65% of respondents in SAP's own study acknowledge they are unsure or do not believe AI is delivering its full potential, even as they report positive returns.[16]

The reconciliation is straightforward. AI delivers measurable productivity gains on narrow use cases, drafting, summarization, Q&A automation, relatively quickly. The transformative impact, redesigning processes, redeploying talent, making better strategic decisions, takes longer and requires organizational change that most companies are still building.

For HR leaders, the practical implication is a dual-speed strategy. Capture quick wins with copilot-level capabilities (Joule answering employee queries, drafting performance reviews, explaining pay) while investing in the data quality, governance, and change management required for agent-level capabilities (automated succession planning, skills-based talent recommendations, workforce analytics on demand).

What this means for the HR operating model

The deeper question is not whether to deploy Joule agents, but what happens to HR when agents handle an increasing share of operational work.

Deloitte and ServiceNow's 2026 enterprise research describes a future where employees increasingly serve as "orchestrators and supervisors of AI agents rather than direct executors of tasks."[18] For HR, this implies a structural shift in how the function allocates its capacity.

Consider the HR Service Center. Today, a significant portion of HR service delivery involves answering repetitive questions, leave policies, benefits eligibility, payroll explanations, onboarding logistics. If an HR Service Agent handles a majority of these queries autonomously, the freed capacity creates space for HR professionals to focus on strategic workforce planning, manager coaching, organizational design, and the contextual employee support that no agent can replicate.

SAP itself is modeling this internally. Its People & Culture Lounge concept, announced in late 2025, provides all 110,000 SAP employees access to one-on-one conversations with HR experts. The rationale is explicit: because technologies like Joule resolve standard inquiries efficiently, personal conversation in complex situations becomes more important, not less.[19]

The pattern is instructive: agents handle volume. Humans handle judgment. The organizations that get the division of labor right will have HR functions that are both more efficient and more human, not one at the expense of the other.

A practical path forward

For SuccessFactors customers evaluating Joule's agentic capabilities, the path forward requires sequencing technology, data, and governance in the right order.

Start with data readiness, not agent deployment. Every agent is only as good as the data it reasons over. If your job architecture is inconsistent, your skills taxonomy is unmaintained, or your performance data is sparse, agents will produce unreliable recommendations, and users will lose trust before the technology gets a chance to prove itself. Clean your Talent Intelligence Hub data. Ensure Growth Portfolios are populated. Validate that role-based permissions are correctly configured.

Activate Joule and capture copilot-level value now. Joule's conversational capabilities, answering employee questions, drafting content, navigating SuccessFactors, are available today and deliver immediate productivity returns. Build user familiarity and organizational comfort with AI in HR workflows before introducing agents that act autonomously.

Deploy the Performance and Goals Agent as your first agent. It is generally available, scoped to a well-understood process, and delivers value that managers will notice immediately. Use it to establish governance patterns, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, feedback mechanisms, monitoring protocols, that you can extend to more complex agents later.

Build governance before you need it. Define which talent decisions require human approval. Establish auditability standards for agent recommendations. Create bias monitoring protocols for agents that influence succession, development, and career pathing. The May 2026 wave of agents will arrive whether you are ready or not.

Plan for Joule Studio when ready-made agents are not enough. If your organization has unique workflows, custom onboarding sequences, industry-specific compliance processes, bespoke talent review cycles, Joule Studio allows you to create custom agents grounded in your business data. But this is a Phase 2 capability. Get the fundamentals right first.

Where to start

The trajectory is unmistakable. Joule is evolving from a conversational interface into an agentic layer that will increasingly participate in, not just support, HR decision-making. The Performance and Goals Agent is the first tangible proof point. The Career and Talent Development, HR Service, People Intelligence, and Payroll agents arriving in 2026 will extend this pattern across the HR lifecycle.

But the technology is ahead of most organizations' readiness. The data is clear: agentic AI governance lags adoption by a wide margin. The organizations that invest now in data quality, permissions architecture, and governance frameworks will be positioned to capture value from agents as they mature. Those that wait for agents to force the issue will spend their time managing risk instead of creating value.

Joule is not a chatbot. Increasingly, it is not even a copilot. It is becoming a new category of HR participant, one that reasons, acts, and learns within the same systems your people use every day. The question for HR leaders is not whether to engage with this shift. It is whether they will shape it, or be shaped by it.

What is your organization's readiness for agentic AI in HR? I'd welcome a conversation, particularly from those who have deployed the Performance and Goals Agent or are planning for the May 2026 wave.

References

 

  1. Deloitte, State of AI in the Enterprise, 2026 Edition
  2. SAP, SAP Announces New Generative AI Assistant Joule
  3. Deloitte, Tech Trends 2026
  4. Deloitte, Agentic AI Strategy, Tech Trends 2026

entry.title: Troubleshooting Guide: "To Be Hired" Status Not Updating in SAP SuccessFactors Manage Positions
entry.content:
Troubleshooting by Scenario:

1. Issues During Import Processes

Scenario A: Job Information Import Not Updating "To Be Hired" Status

When this occurs:

  • Assign or unassign users via job information import

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Follow-up processes not configured

  • Symptoms: Import completes successfully but "To Be Hired" status remains unchanged
  • Solution: Configure HrisSLEventPublishConfig in Manage Data
    1. Navigate to Manage Data
    2. Create "Centralized Services HRIS Event Publishing Configuration" object
    3. If object not found: Configure Object Definitions → Search for "Centralized Services HRIS Event Publishing Configuration" → Make Corrections → Add label → Save
    4. Grant user permissions under Miscellaneous Permissions

Cause 2: Position Management Settings not enabled

  • Symptoms: Imports work but "To Be Hired" status doesn’t adapt
  • Solution: Enable adaptation setting
    1. Go to Position Management Settings → Import tab
    2. Set "Adapt Position 'To Be Hired' Status After Imports" to YES

Scenario B: Termination Import Not Updating Status

When this occurs:

  • Importing termination records
  • API-based termination processing

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Termination imports don’t trigger "To Be Hired" adaptation
  • Reason: System only adapts when position changes occur, not employment status changes
  • Workaround: Manual position record update required after termination

2. Issues with UI Operations

Scenario C: MSS UI Changes Not Updating Position

When this occurs:

  • Using "Change Job and Compensation Info" in MSS
  • Making position changes through Edit button

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Position previously assigned via Job Information History

  • Symptoms: MSS UI changes don’t trigger "To Be Hired" status updates
  • Solution: This is expected behavior - position won’t adapt if originally assigned through Job History
  • Workaround: Use Add New Employee or direct MSS UI for initial assignments

Cause 2: Missing FTE calculation rules

  • Symptoms: Position assignment completes but "To Be Hired" status doesn’t change
  • Solution: Configure FTE calculation business rules
    1. Create business rule using "Calculate Full-Time Equivalent (FTE)" scenario
    2. Set as onChange rule (on custom standard-hours field) OR Set as onSave rule (on jobInfo element)

Cause 3: Missing mandatory fields in position

  • Symptoms: Position change is successful but "To Be Hired" not updated
  • Solution: Complete required position fields
    1. Navigate to Manage Positions
    2. Find affected position → Make Correction
    3. Fill all mandatory fields

Scenario 😧 Job Information History Changes

When this occurs:

  • Making corrections in Job Information History portlet
  • Adding new records in job history

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Changes in Job Information History don’t update "To Be Hired"
  • Solution: Use proper channels for position updates:
    1. Add New Employee wizard for new hires
    2. MSS UI "Change Job and Compensation Info" or Edit button for existing employees

3. Issues with Onboarding and Recruiting Integration

Scenario E: API-Based Onboarding Not Updating Position

When this occurs:

  • Using APIs for onboarding processes

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Position Management doesn’t support follow-up processes for Onboarding API changes
  • Manual Solution: Update position records manually after API-based hiring

Scenario F: Crossboarding/Internal Hire Issues

When this occurs:

  • Initiating crossboarding via Recruiting
  • Internal transfers and promotions

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Cause: "Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities" setting disabled
  • Solution:
    1. Go to Admin Center → Onboarding General Settings
    2. Enable "Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities" option

4. Issues with Multiple Incumbents and FTE Management

Scenario G: Multiple Incumbent Positions

When this occurs:

  • Positions allowing multiple incumbents
  • FTE-based position management

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: positionControlled field not editable

  • Symptoms: FTE calculations incorrect despite correct assignments
  • Solution:
    1. Go to Configure Object Definitions → Position
    2. Set positionControlled field to "editable"

Cause 2: FTE setting configuration

  • Symptoms: Position not updating when FTE appears reached
  • Solution: Adjust Position Management Settings
    1. Navigate to Position Management Settings → General
    2. Change "Set or Reset 'To Be Hired' Status if an Incumbent’s 'FTE' is Changed" to "Always" (instead of "Only if Planned FTE Value is Reached")

Cause 3: Effective dating logic with future hires

  • Symptoms: Current FTE reached but status not updating due to future-dated assignments
  • Solution: Ensure employees assigned with appropriate effective dates that reflect current staffing needs

5. Issues with Position Reclassification

Scenario H: Position Reclassification Creating New Positions

When this occurs:

  • Using Event Reasons with "Position Reclassification" follow-up activity
  • Position updates result in new position creation

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Search setting disabled

  • Solution:
    1. Go to Position Management Settings
    2. Set "Search for Position in Position Reclassification" to "Yes"

Cause 2: Field matching issues

  • Symptoms: System cannot find matching vacant position
  • Solution: Ensure vacant positions have same field values (Department, Division, Custom Fields) or include them in Job-to-Position sync rules

6. Issues with Future Dating and Transition Periods

Scenario I: Future-Dated Hires with transition period

When this occurs:

  • Processing candidates with future start dates
  • Managing transition periods between incumbents

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: "To Be Hired" reflects effective date, not process completion date.  It is currently not possible to update the 'to be hired' status to NO, if a person is terminated in the future and the 'successor' gets assigned to the same position BEFORE the other incumbent is leaving.

To retrigger the "To Be Hired" status adaptation, navigate to Admin Alerts → Position Follow-up Processes Alerts and retrigger the process

If it still fails, you can raise a ticket to product support.

Reference:
Enabling To Be Hired Status Adaptation on the Position (Change from Employee Data Import) | SAP Help Portal
Adapt the To Be Hired Status on the Position (Change from UI) | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Useful steps for admins when migrating from SuccessFactors Legacy People Profile to Full Profile
entry.content:
With Full Profile of the New People Profile on the horizon and being a mandatory change for 1H 2026, here are some practical/admin tips for your transition from Legacy People Profile (LPP) to Full Profile (FP)

1. Clean-up your LPP before running the FP migration job.

Remove any unnecessary, empty, or hidden sections, sub-sections, and blocks before starting the migration. This ensures that the migration job includes only the required portlets, resulting in a cleaner FP environment and eliminating the need for post-migration cleanup.

There are chances that admins may have created these unwanted sections for some testing purposes in the past but never permissioned. So, do a check before initiating the migration job.

2. Understand what is supported and not supported in the FP and how do you identify what migrated across to the FP and what left behind?

Some portlets of LPP such as mini-org chart, performance-potential matrix, functional experience etc. are not supported in FP. So, even when you run the migration job, they are not migrated over to the FP. Therefore, it is important to know what got migrated and not when you ran the job.

How do identify what migrated across to the FP in your system?

When you run the FP migration job from Company Systems & Logo settings page, extract the Download Status file from the Scheduled Job Manager. This gives a comprehensive list of what got migrated and what was left out. It helps you understand old blocks vs new cards in your system. Some screenshots below:

Scheduled Job Manager

Sample of log file

Note: If you have already converted to FP and have done some work on FP, re-running the Migration job would wipe off your FP work and make it a clean slate. So, do it with due diligence.

If you want to switch back to old profile for any testing, you can switch back by unticking the ‘Enable Full Profile for the Latest People Profile’ and revert to FP again by ticking it. This way, your FP work won’t erase.

 

3. Work on the FP Layout:

Once you get a clean transition, and only with wanted categories & cards, now, it is up to the customers to work on the layout. Obviously, you will have an option to rearrange categories and some cards across & within categories. Work on this in consultation with your business and make it suitable as per your business requirements.

Depending on your requirements, you may have to work on RBPs’ here and what you want to see on the cards. You may have to do some RBP tweaks and ordering of the fields so that the most useful information remains on top of the card without needing to dig into the card. (wear a confidentiality lens when doing this)

4. Understand the two-layer permissions:

While your original RBP drives the access to the fields, your category level permissions drives visibility to the categories. Using a RBP Permissions vs Roles Table Report, you can identify which roles has access to the sections/employee views in the LPP and mimic the permissions for Categories in the FP.

For example, from the above-mentioned table report, you identified that the Compensation section (Employee View) in the LPP is permissioned for X, Y, Z roles. Now, in the FP, you can provide the same roles (X, Y, Z) access to the Compensation category and this way you are ensuring that the relevant roles have access to the categories in the FP. (Once you start working on this, you will get a hang on this. If you need any help on the report, feel free to contact me and happy to help).

5. Use Configuration Transport Centre (CTC) to migrate your changes across SF instances. (This is truly a life saver)

Believe me, it is indeed a life saver!

Assuming you have started & developed all your work in a test instance before moving to production, using a CTC will save your time, effort and an efficient way to copy your changes across the instances.

Through CTC, your entire FP config will copy across the instances. The ordering of the categories, cards, category permissions, any text changes config will all copy across.

In my experience, the config in the General settings i.e., Header fields, enable latest Editing & History UI etc. of the FP did not copy across, but these are just couple of ticks, so that worries least for any admins/customers. You can manually tick them once your category level config is copied over.

6. Finally, some quick unit testing to make sure everything is done properly!

entry.title: List of Employee Central & Employee Central Payroll Guide Updates for the 1H 2026 Release
entry.content:
In SAP SuccessFactors User Assistance, we’ve been working on improving your information experience.

Latest People Profile

We have made more enhancements to the latest People Profile, including redesigned Editing UI and History UI. For more information, refer to Latest People Profile. 

Country/Region-Specific Dependent Configuration

We have added a new section to the Implementing Dependents guide to help you take advantage of the new country/region-specific dependent configuration feature. For more information, refer to Implementing Dependents.

Understanding Settings for Manage Business Configuration

We have added a new topic to the Setting Up Data Models and Managing Configuration guide to understand more details about the features you can enable in your system. We have added more information about dependencies between the settings as well as links to the related guides. For more information, refer to topic in the guide.

Release Center

The Release Center adds the What’s New Viewer content to the Admin Center. It automatically filters the What's New Viewer content based on your tenant's configuration and gives you tools to collaborate with other administrators in your company.

For more information, refer to the  Release Center Adds What's New to Admin Center | SAP Help Portal WNV topic and the What’s New, Now Relevant: Introducing Release Cent... - SAP Community blog.

Admin Page Revamp

We have revamped a few of the admin pages, for example, Company Settings, to meet SAP user interface standards. The pages have all the same settings and functions. For more information, refer to Administrator Pages Revamp for 1H 2026 | SAP Help Portal.

Deprecation Guides for Employee Central

Guide

Change

Details

Implementing Service Center in Employee Central

In Deprecation

This feature is replaced with Enterprise Service Management. For more information, refer to Enterprise Service Management

Using Service Center (End-User) 

 

This feature is replaced with Enterprise Service Management. For more information, refer to Enterprise Service Management.

Cross-System Workflow for SAP SuccessFactors

 In Deprecation

The feature is being deprecated.
